title: The Compulsion Engine: What Founders Chase When Happiness Isn't the Goal

source.original: David Senra's Conversation with Marc Andreessen (@pmarca) – Founders, Tech as Progress, and the Zero Introspection Mindset

body_html:
Most people optimize for some version of happiness—comfort, security, freedom from stress. Founders, as Andreessen describes them, are playing an entirely different game. Their motivation sits below the conscious layer, closer to compulsion than calculation. They build because they must, not because they've run a cost-benefit analysis on fulfillment.

This distinction matters because it reframes burnout, sacrifice, and obsession not as pathologies but as features of a particular operating system. When conventional psychology labels relentless work as unhealthy, it's applying a framework designed for people who want balance. The founders Andreessen admires don't want balance—they want to reshape the landscape they see in front of them.

The idea that motivation transcends happiness also explains why external rewards often fail to satisfy the builder archetype. Money, status, and recognition are byproducts, not objectives. The founder who sells a company for a billion dollars and immediately starts another isn't irrational—they're simply responding to an internal signal that has nothing to do with net worth.

This has real implications for how we think about founder well-being. If the drive is fundamentally non-hedonic, then optimizing for happiness metrics will always miss the point. The more useful question isn't are they happy but are they building at the edge of what they're capable of.
